The context around that is important. The author was an introvert and very shy, the only nonwhite student, feeling threatened by the tall athletes. She spent time alone reading and writing. Ms. Leibfried noticed her interest in reading and writing and took time to make her less shy. Also, she gave her reading suggestions. So the teacher was trying to help the student come out of her shell. The act of reciting in front of the class would be a way to build her confidence. The author was nervous, but after doing it, the class applauded, and she felt confident for the first time. Looking at the options: A) To test memory. The passage doesn't mention anything about memory. The focus was on reading and presenting a passage, but the teacher's intention seems more about confidence and engagement. It's not framed as a challenge to gain respect but as an opportunity to express herself. C) To deepen understanding of "The Bell Jar." The author did pick a passage she thought was best, but the teacher's suggestion was to do something different from a report. The main point seems to be about the student's growth, not just academic understanding. D) To guide her to build a good relationship with others. The teacher wanted to help her be less shy, which could involve social interaction.
